groups.name: Broad Equity
groups.description: Diversified equity funds spanning US and international-developed markets across market cap, style (blend/value/growth), and broad geography (US, foreign, global, developed-region). Analysis focuses on index tracking, style purity, peer-group comparison within cap/style/region, and broad-market beta behavior. Excludes single-sector, single-theme, and emerging-market-country funds — those live in sector-thematic-equity.

groups.name: Sector, Thematic & Emerging-Market Equity
groups.description: Equity funds with a narrow, specific exposure — a single sector or industry (technology, health, financials, real estate, energy, infrastructure, etc.), a theme (digital-asset equities, precious-metals equities, MLPs, natural resources), or a single emerging-market country/region (China, India, Latin America, Diversified EM). The common thread is concentrated, non-diversified exposure where the buy decision is about timing or conviction on that specific corner of the market. Analysis focuses on sector/theme/region cyclicality, concentration risk, top-holding exposure, sub-bucket diversification (single-country, single-currency), and peer comparison within the same narrow exposure.

groups.name: Leveraged & Inverse Trading
groups.description: Daily-rebalanced leveraged, inverse, or multi-asset leveraged funds. Analysis focuses on daily-reset path dependency (decay), suitability strictly as short-term trading vehicles, accuracy of the daily-leverage objective, and holding-period warnings.

groups.name: Commodities & Digital Assets
groups.description: Direct-exposure funds where the wrapper simply holds (or tracks) a thing rather than running a manager strategy — physical commodities (broad basket, precious metals, energy), digital assets (spot Bitcoin, spot Ether, crypto baskets), single currencies, and currency/crypto pair-trade products. Analysis focuses on tracking quality versus the underlying spot price, contango / roll cost (for futures-based commodity funds), custody and structural integrity (for crypto), correlation with equities, and how the position interacts with the rest of a portfolio (inflation hedge, dollar hedge, speculative satellite).

groups.name: Derivative Income & Alternative Strategies
groups.description: Non-traditional return-stream funds. Two sub-families share one decision framework: (1) options-engineered payoff funds — covered-call / derivative-income (JEPI, QYLD, SPYI), defined-outcome / buffer (Innovator, FT Vest), equity-hedged collar products — where the fund mechanically sells or buys options to engineer yield, a cap, or a floor; (2) hedge-fund-style strategy funds — long-short equity, market-neutral, event-driven, multi-strategy, macro trading, managed futures / systematic trend, relative-value arbitrage, multialternative, volatility / downside hedge — where a manager runs a process aimed at uncorrelated returns or downside protection. Analysis focuses on whether the strategy delivered on its mandate (yield + cap + floor for options funds; low correlation + downside protection + risk-adjusted return for strategy funds), distribution composition / tax character, and risk-adjusted return against the appropriate benchmark — not absolute return vs the S&P 500.

groups.name: Allocation & Target-Date
groups.description: Funds with a prescribed asset-mix mandate — static allocation (moderate/aggressive/conservative), tactical allocation, global allocation variants, and all target-date glide-path funds. Analysis focuses on mandate adherence, tactical or glide-path execution, drawdown reduction vs pure-equity peers, and risk-adjusted returns within the allocation peer group.

groups.name: Fixed Income — Investment Grade
groups.description: High-quality, low-default-risk fixed income. Includes US government bonds (Treasuries, agency MBS, TIPS, target maturity), investment-grade corporate bonds (short, intermediate, long, core, core-plus), global investment-grade bonds (USD-hedged and unhedged), and cash-equivalents (ultrashort bond, money market, prime money market). Also includes investment-grade municipal bond funds — national and single-state (CA, NY, NJ, MA, OH, MN) — because their decision framework (duration + rate path + tax-equivalent yield versus comparable taxable peers) sits alongside the rest of IG fixed income. Main risk = rates (duration). Default risk is structurally low. Analysis focuses on duration / rate sensitivity, yield (with TEY framing for muni at a stated bracket), benchmark tracking, and resilience across interest-rate environments.

groups.name: Fixed Income — Credit & Income
groups.description: Credit-driven, income-focused bond funds where the yield is paid for taking real default or subordination risk — not for taking rate risk. Includes high-yield corporate bonds, bank loans (senior-secured floating-rate), CLO tranche funds (Securitized Bond – Focused), preferred stock, convertibles, emerging-market debt (both hard-currency and local-currency), multisector bond, nontraditional bond, private debt, broad credit, and high-yield municipal funds (because their risk profile is credit, not the IG-muni TEY story). Main risk = default and spread widening, with secondary duration exposure for the longer-dated members. Analysis focuses on credit quality, default risk, spread behavior, downside during credit stress (2020 COVID, 2008 GFC), and yield-versus-default-risk trade-off.

categoryInstructions.PerformanceAndReturns: Many funds in the Defined Outcome category use structured option overlays (typically a buffer or floor plus a cap, sized via FLEX options) to deliver a defined risk / return band over a fixed outcome period. If that fits this ETF, read past performance through these levers and try to source them from the issuer fact sheet / prospectus: outcome period (typically 1 year, sometimes quarterly / 2-year / 3-year), buffer level (e.g. 9 / 15 / 30%), upside cap (the performance ceiling net of fees), cap reset methodology (how the new cap is set at the start of each outcome period given prevailing vol), laddered vs single-ticker (a single-vintage fund delivers one cap window; a laddered series spreads outcome periods), option structure (buffer absorbs a defined first-loss layer; floor caps losses at a fixed level), active ratchet / dynamic reset (whether the cap moves with realised gains), and holding period requirement (mid-period buyers / sellers do NOT get the stated buffer + cap). Compare total return to the underlying equity index across full outcome periods — partial-period performance is structurally meaningless because the buffer / cap is only realised at outcome end. Skip any lever you cannot source confidently. If the fund deviates from this template (covered-call only, managed-futures, multi-alternative), analyse on its actual mandate instead. Only highlight what is relevant to this ETF.
categoryInstructions.CostEfficiencyAndTeam: Many funds in the Defined Outcome category use FLEX-option overlays to deliver a defined risk / return band over a fixed outcome period. If that fits this ETF, the cost picture is more than expense ratio — FLEX-option bid-ask, cap-reset slippage at the start of each outcome period, and (in taxable accounts) less-favourable tax treatment on option-driven income all matter. Cap reset methodology and the laddered vs single-ticker design directly drive turnover and transaction friction; a quarterly-reset laddered series will carry more ongoing trading cost than a once-a-year single-ticker. Holding period requirement matters operationally — mid-period buys / sells get a different exposure than the stated buffer + cap, so retail must understand that the fund is only "defined" when held across the full outcome period. Try to source these from the issuer fact sheet / prospectus before estimating. If the fund deviates from this strategy, focus on the manager team's track record and the cost of running that specific strategy. Only highlight levers that are relevant to this ETF.
categoryInstructions.RiskAnalysis: Many funds in the Defined Outcome category use FLEX-option overlays to deliver a defined risk / return band over a fixed outcome period. If that fits this ETF, the real risks are mid-period mark-to-market dislocation and the upside cap, not equity-style volatility — read drawdown and capture ratios against the underlying equity index, not the S&P 500 directly. Note outcome period, buffer level, upside cap, option structure (buffer absorbs a defined first-loss layer; floor caps losses at a fixed level), active ratchet / dynamic reset, and the holding period requirement. Mid-period drawdowns can briefly exceed the stated buffer because the option payoff is only realised at outcome end — flag this so retail understands the on-screen NAV does not necessarily honour the buffer until the outcome period closes. If the fund deviates from this template (covered-call only, managed-futures, multi-alternative), the risk read is correlation to equities and crisis-period drawdown — judge whether the manager delivered the uncorrelated returns the mandate promises. Only highlight what is relevant — skip a lever you cannot source confidently.
categoryInstructions.FuturePerformanceOutlook: Many funds in the Defined Outcome category use FLEX-option overlays to deliver a defined risk / return band over a fixed outcome period. If that fits this ETF, the forward read hinges on where the fund sits inside the current outcome period and what the next cap reset will look like — outcome period, buffer level, the current upside cap and how much of it remains, cap reset methodology (vol-driven), laddered vs single-ticker, option structure, active ratchet / dynamic reset, and the holding period requirement drive whether forward returns are realistic. High-vol regimes tend to set higher caps and richer buffers at reset; low-vol regimes tighten caps. Note tax treatment in taxable accounts (option-driven income is typically less tax-efficient than qualified dividends) and remind the reader that the fund is only "defined" if held across the full outcome period. If the fund deviates from this template, the forward read is whether the current macro / volatility regime favours the manager's actual style — skip the defined-outcome levers. Only highlight what is relevant to this ETF.

categoryInstructions.PerformanceAndReturns: Many funds in the Derivative Income category run a covered-call or other options-engineered payoff strategy. If that fits this ETF, read past performance through these levers and try to source them from the issuer fact sheet / prospectus / Mor / etf.com: option structure (calls / puts / collars and which index or basket is overwritten), % of portfolio overwritten, call writing frequency (daily / weekly / monthly), days to expiration (DTE) of the options sold, strike selection methodology (delta-based vs fixed distance vs premium target), volatility regime sensitivity (how the strategy behaved across different VIX levels), roll methodology (when and how losing positions are managed), notional coverage ratio, and the NAV erosion track record. Compare total return (with distributions reinvested) to the underlying index — a flat or shrinking price-only NAV beside a positive total return is the tell that distributions are partly return of capital, not real earnings. If the fund is a long-short / market-neutral / managed-futures / multi-strategy product instead, ignore these levers and analyse on its own mandate. Only highlight what is relevant — skip a lever if you do not have a credible source for it.
categoryInstructions.CostEfficiencyAndTeam: Many funds in the Derivative Income category run a covered-call or other options-engineered payoff strategy. If that fits this ETF, the full cost picture is more than expense ratio — bid-ask on the options, slippage from rolling, and (in taxable accounts) less-favourable tax treatment on option-premium income vs qualified dividends all matter. Note call writing frequency (daily / weekly / monthly), days to expiration (DTE), strike selection methodology (delta-based vs fixed distance vs premium target), and roll methodology — they directly drive turnover and transaction friction. Try to source these from the issuer fact sheet / prospectus before guessing. If the fund is a long-short / market-neutral / managed-futures / multi-strategy product instead, focus on the manager team's track record and the cost of running that specific strategy. Only highlight levers that are relevant to this ETF.
categoryInstructions.RiskAnalysis: Many funds in the Derivative Income category run a covered-call or other options-engineered payoff strategy. If that fits this ETF, the real risks are upside-capping plus NAV erosion, not equity-style volatility — read drawdown and capture ratios against the underlying index, not the S&P 500 directly. Note volatility regime sensitivity (how the strategy behaves at different VIX levels), notional coverage ratio, roll methodology when positions move against the fund, and the NAV erosion track record (a steadily declining price-only NAV next to a positive total return is structural erosion). If the fund is a long-short / market-neutral / managed-futures / multi-strategy product instead, the risk read is correlation to equities and crisis-period drawdown — judge whether the manager delivered the uncorrelated returns the mandate promises. Only highlight what is relevant — skip a lever if you have no credible source for it.
categoryInstructions.FuturePerformanceOutlook: Many funds in the Derivative Income category run a covered-call or other options-engineered payoff strategy. If that fits this ETF, the forward read hinges on the volatility regime and the fund's structural levers — option structure, % overwritten, call frequency, DTE, strike methodology, and roll discipline drive whether forward distributions can hold up. Low-vol regimes shrink the option premium the fund can harvest; high-vol regimes grow it but also widen roll risk. Note tax treatment in taxable accounts (option-premium income is typically less tax-efficient than qualified dividends) and the fund's NAV erosion track record as the forward base case. If the fund is a long-short / market-neutral / managed-futures / multi-strategy product instead, the forward read is whether the current macro / volatility regime favours the manager's style — skip the covered-call levers. Only highlight what is relevant to this ETF.

categoryInstructions.PerformanceAndReturns: Many funds in the Equity Hedged category run a put / collar / spread overlay that caps downside while keeping most of the equity upside. If that fits this ETF, read past performance through these levers and try to source them from the issuer fact sheet / prospectus / Mor: hedge construction type (long puts vs put spreads vs collars), downside protection range (e.g. 9 / 15 / 20% buffer or floor), floor level or put-strike distance (% OTM), hedge laddering structure (single expiry vs laddered maturities), hedge financing method (cost of puts paid outright, financed by selling calls, or via spreads), hedged notional percentage, hedge reset frequency, upside capping approach, and volatility regime sensitivity (how the hedge cost / payoff shifts across VIX levels). Compare total return to the underlying equity index — a hedged strategy should lag in bull windows and outperform in drawdowns; if it trailed in both directions over a full cycle, the hedge structure was likely mistimed or too expensive. Skip any lever you cannot confidently source. If the fund is actually a covered-call / market-neutral / managed-futures product despite the category label, analyse on that mandate instead. Only highlight what is relevant to this ETF.
categoryInstructions.CostEfficiencyAndTeam: Many funds in the Equity Hedged category run a put / collar / spread overlay. If that fits this ETF, the cost picture is more than expense ratio — premium paid on the puts, options bid-ask, roll slippage, and (in taxable accounts) less-favourable tax treatment on option-driven income vs qualified dividends all matter. Hedge financing method (paid outright vs financed by selling calls vs spreads) and hedge reset frequency directly drive turnover and transaction friction; laddered structures usually carry steadier but higher ongoing transaction costs than single-expiry hedges. Try to source these from the issuer fact sheet / prospectus before estimating. If the fund deviates from this strategy (covered-call only, managed-futures, multi-alternative), focus on the manager team's track record and the cost of running that specific strategy. Only highlight levers that are relevant to this ETF.
categoryInstructions.RiskAnalysis: Many funds in the Equity Hedged category run a put / collar / spread overlay. If that fits this ETF, the real risks are basis / timing of the hedge and the cap on upside, not equity-style volatility — read drawdown and capture ratios against the underlying equity index, not the S&P 500 directly. Note hedge construction type, downside protection range, floor level / put-strike distance (% OTM), hedged notional percentage, hedge reset frequency, and volatility regime sensitivity (how the hedge cost / payoff shifts across VIX levels). If the hedge expired before a drawdown was realised or did not reset in time, the protection was theoretical — check that historical drawdowns landed near the stated buffer / floor, not well beyond it. If the fund is actually a covered-call / market-neutral / managed-futures product, the risk read is correlation to equities and crisis-period drawdown — judge whether the manager delivered the uncorrelated returns the mandate promises. Only highlight what is relevant — skip a lever you cannot source confidently.
categoryInstructions.FuturePerformanceOutlook: Many funds in the Equity Hedged category run a put / collar / spread overlay. If that fits this ETF, the forward read hinges on the current volatility regime and the fund's structural levers — hedge construction type, downside protection range, floor level / put-strike distance, hedge laddering structure, hedge financing method, hedged notional percentage, hedge reset frequency, upside capping approach, and volatility regime sensitivity drive whether the hedge will pay off cheaply or expensively across the next 6–12 months. Low-vol regimes make puts cheap (good for forward protection, but also signal complacency); high-vol regimes make puts expensive and force the fund to give up more upside via the financing leg. Note tax treatment in taxable accounts (option-driven income is typically less tax-efficient than qualified dividends). If the fund deviates from this strategy, the forward read is whether the current macro / volatility regime favours the manager's actual style — skip the hedged-equity levers. Only highlight what is relevant to this ETF.
